title: Injecting Noise
concept: Injecting noise adds small randomness to inputs or hidden representations during training so the network stops depending on perfectly clean signals and learns features that survive minor corruption.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What must stay intact for text augmentation to be useful?

options.text: The same typo in every sentence.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Augmentation helps when wording changes but the important answer stays the same.

cards.title: Practice with a little static
cards.body: If you only ever train in perfectly quiet conditions, even a tiny hiss can throw you off later. Neural networks can be the same way. A little controlled noise during training can prepare them for messy real-world inputs.

cards.title: What noise injection does
cards.body: Noise injection means adding small randomness to the input or to internal activations during training. This nudges the network away from depending on one perfectly clean signal and toward features that remain useful even when the data is slightly disturbed.

cards.title: Learning to shoot in a breeze
cards.body: A basketball player who practices only in a silent indoor gym may struggle outdoors in the wind. Practicing with a little breeze builds a steadier shot. Noise injection does something similar for a model.

cards.title: Sensors are rarely perfect
cards.body: A factory sensor model may see tiny jitters, blur, or measurement wobble in the real world. If training includes small input noise or mild noise inside hidden representations, the network is less shocked when those imperfections appear later.

cards.title: Noise is not the same as sabotage
cards.body: A common myth is that adding noise always ruins the data. Too much noise can hurt, yes. But small, realistic noise can act like a regularizer by forcing the network to stop clinging to brittle exact values.

cards.title: Perfectly clean training vs. slightly noisy training
cards.body: The goal is not chaos. The goal is mild disturbance that teaches stability.

left.text:
Model may depend on exact values.
Small real-world corruption can cause sharp drops.

right.text:
Model practices through small disturbances.
Predictions often become more robust.

exercises.prompt: Why might adding small noise during training help a model?

options.text: It encourages the model to rely on patterns that survive small disturbances instead of exact clean values.
It guarantees the training loss instantly becomes zero.
It removes the need for labels.
It permanently freezes hidden activations after one batch.

exercises.explain: Right. Noise can act like a gentle stress test, rewarding features that keep working under small corruption.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Small, realistic noise can improve robustness, even though extreme noise would usually hurt.
exercises.explain: True. The useful version is mild and realistic, not overwhelming corruption.

exercises.sentence: Noise injection teaches the model not to rely on perfectly ___ inputs.
exercises.explain: Clean is correct. Real-world data often includes a little mess, so the model should not depend on flawless signals.

exercises.prompt: Which plan sounds most like sensible noise injection?

options.text: Add a small amount of realistic jitter during training so the model learns stable features.
Destroy the input so completely that the true pattern disappears.
Use no labels because the noise will teach the task by itself.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Good noise injection is mild enough that the task still makes sense.

exercises.prompt: A sensor-based defect detector works in the lab but becomes unreliable on the factory floor, where readings have tiny jitters and small disturbances. What is the best explanation for trying noise injection during training?

options.text: Adding mild noise to inputs or hidden representations can train the network to rely on sturdier patterns that still hold when real measurements are slightly messy.
The best fix is to make the training data perfectly cleaner than reality so the model never sees disturbances.
Noise works by changing the correct labels whenever the input jitters.
A robust model should memorize each exact sensor value instead of learning broader patterns.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Noise injection helps close the gap between idealized training conditions and the slightly messy signals that show up in real deployment.
